metadata.summary: GitHub Actions workflow anatomy: trigger types, concurrency groups, job dependencies (needs: DAG), environment protection, scheduled workflows, and repository setup checklist for branch protection.

content.title: GitHub Actions Workflow Structure
content.summary: Model every workflow as a DAG of jobs using needs: before writing YAML. Assign concurrency groups per trigger context (PR builds cancel on new push; deployment jobs do not). Use environment: for all deploy targets so secrets are scoped and approvals are trackable. Run scheduled workflows at low-traffic UTC times and always add workflow_dispatch as a manual escape hatch.

why.p: The most common structural mistake agents make is writing five sequential jobs that could be parallel — missing needs: relationships — or the inverse, wiring needs: dependencies that create unnecessary serialisation. Modelling the job DAG first (job, needs[], trigger, runs-on, permissions, timeout) prevents both mistakes. GitHub renders the dependency graph in the Actions UI and uses it for status-check routing.

Concurrency groups prevent two common failure modes: (1) redundant CI builds on rapid push accumulate runners and queue depth, slowing every contributor; (2) a new push to main cancels an in-flight production deployment because the group was set globally. The fix is one group per (workflow, ref) for PR builds and one group per (workflow, environment) for deploys.

list.item: Structuring a new workflow from scratch — model the DAG before writing YAML.
Diagnosing slow CI — look for unnecessary sequential jobs and missing cache/artifact handoffs.
Adding a new environment (staging, production, preview) — wire up GitHub Environment for scoped secrets and approvals.
Setting up scheduled maintenance (security scans, dependency updates, DB backups) — use schedule + workflow_dispatch.
Single-step workflows — a one-job workflow needs no DAG analysis.
Local-only prototyping without a GitHub remote — use act for local testing instead.

rule.statement: Design the job dependency graph (needs: relationships) as a DAG BEFORE writing YAML. Jobs without needs: run in parallel; jobs with needs: run after their dependencies. Never serialize jobs that can run in parallel.
rule.rationale: Sequential jobs on GHA-hosted runners waste wall-clock time and runner-minutes. A lint + test + build chain that could be lint → (test || build) in parallel cuts CI time by 30-50%.

rule.statement: Concurrency groups for PR/push CI workflows MUST use group: ${{ github.workflow }}-${{ github.ref }} with cancel-in-progress: true. Concurrency groups for deployment workflows MUST use group: deploy-${{ inputs.environment }} with cancel-in-progress: false.
rule.rationale: A shared global concurrency group cancels production deploys when a new commit to a feature branch triggers a CI run with the same group name. Per-ref grouping for CI and per-environment grouping for deploys prevents cross-contamination.

rule.statement: Every scheduled workflow MUST also have a workflow_dispatch trigger so operators can run it manually outside the cron schedule during incidents.
rule.rationale: A scheduled backup or security scan that only runs at 02:00 UTC cannot be manually triggered after an incident that happened at 14:00. workflow_dispatch is a zero-cost safety valve.

section.name: workflow-types
section.p: GHA supports six workflow trigger types, each suited to different pipeline stages.

list.item: CI — triggers: push + pull_request. Purpose: build, lint, test. Cancel redundant runs with concurrency.
CD — trigger: push to main. Purpose: deploy to environments. Requires environment protection gates.
Release — trigger: push tags (v*). Purpose: publish packages, create GitHub Release, build multi-arch Docker image.
Scheduled — trigger: schedule (cron) + workflow_dispatch. Purpose: security scans, dependency updates, DB backups, artifact cleanup.
Reusable — trigger: workflow_call. Purpose: shared deploy/build logic called from multiple repos. See gha-deployment-patterns.
Manual — trigger: workflow_dispatch. Purpose: on-demand deployments, incident response, one-off tasks.

example.title: CI concurrency — cancel redundant PR builds
example.note: When a new commit is pushed to a PR, the previous CI run is cancelled. Each PR and branch has its own group.

example.title: Deploy concurrency — never cancel in-flight deploys
example.note: A new commit queues behind the in-flight deploy instead of killing it. Use per-environment group so staging and prod are independent.

example.title: Global concurrency group cancels prod deploys
example.code: # ALL workflows — including prod deploy — share one group concurrency: group: ${{ github.workflow }} cancel-in-progress: true
example.note: A feature branch CI run cancels an active production deployment. Both workflows share the same name-derived group.

section.name: complete-ci-workflow
section.p: A full CI workflow for Node.js: lint → (test matrix + build) in parallel → upload artifact.

example.title: CI pipeline with parallel test and build
Scheduled maintenance with workflow_dispatch escape hatch

section.name: monorepo-path-filters
section.p: Monorepos benefit from path-filtered CI so only changed packages are built and tested.

example.title: Monorepo CI with dorny/paths-filter
example.code:
jobs: changes: name: Detect Changes runs-on: ubuntu-latest outputs: frontend: ${{ steps.filter.outputs.frontend }} backend: ${{ steps.filter.outputs.backend }} steps: - uses: actions/checkout@v4 - uses: dorny/paths-filter@v3 id: filter with: filters: | frontend: - 'packages/frontend/**' - 'packages/shared/**' backend: - 'packages/backend/**' - 'packages/shared/**'
frontend: needs: changes if: needs.changes.outputs.frontend == 'true' runs-on: ubuntu-latest steps: - uses: actions/checkout@v4 - uses: actions/setup-node@v4 with: { node-version: '20', cache: 'npm' } - run: npm ci && npm run build --workspace=packages/frontend - run: npm test --workspace=packages/frontend
backend: needs: changes if: needs.changes.outputs.backend == 'true' runs-on: ubuntu-latest steps: - uses: actions/checkout@v4 - uses: actions/setup-python@v5 with: { python-version: '3.12' } - run: cd packages/backend && pip install -r requirements.txt && pytest

section.name: repository-setup
section.p: Before the first workflow runs, configure the repository correctly in GitHub Settings.

list.item: Create .github/workflows/ directory in the repo root.
Enable branch protection on main: require status checks (CI), require PR reviews, require branches to be up to date.
Create Environments (staging, production) under Settings → Environments with required reviewers and environment secrets.
Add all required secrets under Settings → Secrets and Variables → Actions.
Set Actions permissions under Settings → Actions to restrict which actions are allowed (preferably: allow only from the same org + verified Marketplace creators).

section.name: action-versions
section.p: Canonical action versions as of 2026. Always pin to SHA — these version numbers are for reference only.

list.item: actions/checkout v4 — standard checkout.
actions/setup-node v4 — Node.js setup with built-in cache.
actions/setup-python v5 — Python setup.
actions/cache v4 — new cache service (rewritten Feb 2025).
docker/build-push-action v6 — multi-platform builds.
docker/setup-buildx-action v3 — Buildx setup.

checklist.check: Job DAG modelled before writing YAML — no unnecessary sequential jobs.
CI concurrency uses per-ref group with cancel-in-progress: true.
Deploy concurrency uses per-environment group with cancel-in-progress: false.
Every deploy job uses environment: name with a GitHub Environment configured.
Scheduled cron runs at low-traffic UTC hour.
Branch protection rules configured: required CI checks, required reviews.
fetch-depth: 1 used on checkout unless full history is needed (e.g., changelog generation).
Monorepo workflows use path filtering to avoid rebuilding unchanged packages.
